title: podman logs and podman exec not working when using systemd LogNamespace
body:
### Issue Description

Creating a container and having it orchestrated by systemd won't work as expected if you edit the `.service` file to [include a `LogNamespace`](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd-journald.service.html#Journal%20Namespaces). A LogNamespace allows us to separate logs from services and quickly viewing them by using `journalctl --namespace=` and is very useful if you have noisy containers that would quickly fill up journald storage.

In a scenario where you appended a `LogNamespace` to a service file, you will no longer be able to:

1. Execute `podman logs` on that container -- the output will be empty
2. Execute `podman exec` on that container if it also has `--user` set -- The command will fail with the message `Error: unable to find user XXX: no matching entries in passwd file`, unless you specify a UID instead, e.g.: `podman exec -tiu 0 mycontainer bash`
3. Have your container's healthcheck enabled if it has a `--user` set, as that will also fail under the same issue above (no matching entries in passwd file)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman create --name mycontainer --rm -e MARIADB_ALLOW_EMPTY_ROOT_PASSWORD=1 --user=mysql --health-interval 10s --health-cmd "healthcheck.sh --innodb_initialized" docker.io/library/mariadb:10.11.4`
2. `cd /etc/systemd/system && podman generate systemd --new --files --name --container-prefix=ct mycontainer`
3. Edit the file `mycontainer.service` and append the line `LogNamespace=ct` right after the `[Service]` line
4. `podman rm mycontainer`  (just for cleanup)
5. `systemctl daemon-reload`
6. `systemctl start ct-mycontainer`

Now, for testing:
1. `podman logs mycontainer`  -- will be empty, although you can see logs with `journalctl --namespace=ct -efu ct-mycontainer`
2. `podman exec -ti mycontainer bash` -- will fail
3. `podman exec -ti -u root mycontainer bash` -- will also fail, unless you pass a `0` instead of `root`
4. `journalctl -f` will show failures when doing healthchecks, usually `unable to find user root: no matching entries in passwd file`

### Describe the results you received

Effective usage of `podman logs`, `podman exec` and health checks severely impacted, although there are workarounds.

### Describe the results you expected

Have them all working.

### Additional information

comments.body:
I have not looked in the systemd code but the docs say:

> Internally, journal namespaces are implemented through Linux mount namespacing and over-mounting the directory that contains the relevant AF_UNIX sockets used for logging in the unit's mount namespace. Since mount namespaces are used this setting disconnects propagation of mounts from the unit's processes to the host, similarly to how ReadOnlyPaths= and similar settings describe above work. Journal namespaces may hence not be used for services that need to establish mount points on the host.

Podman has to manage mount points so this cannot work.
